title: Start Planning Early
description: The earlier you start, the more options you have. This is when you set the foundation.

items.task: Define your purpose and post-military goals
items.details: What do you want your life to look like after the uniform? Write it down. Share it with someone who matters.

items.task: Start or continue your degree using Tuition Assistance
items.details: Use TA ($250/credit hour, $4,500/year) for your bachelor's. Don't touch the GI Bill yet. Find schools that match TA rates.

items.task: Open and fund your TSP account
items.details: If you're BRS, contribute at least 5% to get full government match. Start early — compound interest is your biggest weapon.
Every dollar in debt payments is a dollar that isn't building your future. Attack high-interest debt first.
Network with people in fields you're interested in. Your post-military career starts being built now.
Requires 6+ years of service and 4-year commitment. Transfer at least 1 month to each dependent to get them in the system. You can't add dependents after separation.
Every time something hurts, go to medical. Get it in your record. This is the foundation for your VA disability claim.
NPS, AFIT, or civilian grad school on the military's dime. They pay tuition AND your salary. If available, take it.

title: Get Serious
description: Transition is no longer theoretical. Start making concrete moves.

items.task: Attend TAP (Transition Assistance Program) — Individualized Initial Counseling
items.details: One-on-one session with a TAP counselor. Complete self-assessment, begin your Individual Transition Plan (ITP), initiate DD Form 2648, determine your tier level.
SkillBridge lets you work for a civilian employer during your last 180 days while keeping military pay. Start researching companies and programs now — command approval takes time.
If your role gives you access to cert courses (GPEN, CISSP, PMP, etc.), take them now. That window might be shorter than you think.
Annual expenses × 25 = your number. Factor in pension, VA disability, TSP, and investments. Know exactly where you stand.

items.task: Research state veteran benefits where you plan to live
items.details: Property tax exemptions, education benefits for dependents, employment preferences — these vary wildly by state.

title: Execute Your Plan
description: One year to go. Time to start executing, not just planning.

items.details: Three mandatory days: DoD/DHS Transition Day (Managing Your Transition, MOC Crosswalk, Financial Planning), VA Day, and DOL Employment Workshop. Don't blow these off — actually engage.
Translate your military experience into civilian language. No jargon. Focus on outcomes and impact, not duties. Get it reviewed by civilians in your target industry.
Professional headshot (no uniform), civilian-friendly headline, translated experience. Start connecting with people in your target field.

items.task: Request your SMART transcript / military training records
items.details: Your military training converts to college credits. Get your transcript and see what transfers. Army: AARTS. Navy/Marines: SMART. Air Force: CCAF.

items.task: Review your personnel records for accuracy
items.details: Check your service record, awards, qualifications. Fix errors now — it's much harder after you're out.
Submit your SkillBridge application with enough lead time for command approval. Some commands need 6+ months notice.

title: BDD Window Opens
description: This is critical — your VA disability claims window opens NOW.

items.details: Benefits Delivery at Discharge (BDD) window: 180-90 days before separation. File NOW. Use a VSO (free). This is not optional — your rating can mean thousands per month tax-free for the rest of your life.

items.task: Connect with a Veterans Service Organization (VSO)
items.details: DAV, VFW, American Legion — they provide free claims representatives. They know the system. Let them help you file.
Compensation & Pension exams are how the VA rates your conditions. Don't miss them. Don't minimize symptoms. Be honest about how your conditions affect your daily life.
180 days of working for a civilian employer at full military pay. This is your tryout — and theirs. Treat it like the job interview it is.
Many employers want someone who can start in 2-4 weeks. Time your applications accordingly. Too early and they'll pass. Too late and you'll have a gap.
TAMP (180 days free TRICARE after separation), TRICARE Retiree (if 20-year), VA healthcare enrollment, CHCBP, employer insurance. Know what covers you and your family.

title: Final Preparations
description: BDD window is closing. Finish filing claims. Lock in your plan.

items.task: LAST CHANCE: File any remaining VA disability claims
items.details: BDD window closes at 90 days before separation. If you haven't filed, do it TODAY. After this window, the process takes much longer.
Review of your Career Readiness Standards (CRS). Confirm all requirements are met. Get warm handovers for anything outstanding.
If PCS'ing on terminal leave: schedule movers, arrange housing, update DEERS address. If retiring: plan your final move carefully — you only get one.
If you receive a 100% P&T rating before separation, file Ch. 35 for your dependents immediately. Separate 36-month entitlement for each eligible dependent.

items.task: Apply for state veteran education benefits
items.details: Conroy (MD), Hazlewood (TX), and dozens of other state programs. Research what's available in the state you're moving to. Apply before school enrollment deadlines.

items.task: Set up your financial safety net
items.details: Have 3-6 months of expenses in savings. Know exactly when your first pension check, VA disability payment, and civilian paycheck will arrive. Plan for the gap.

title: Wrapping Up
description: You're almost there. Tie up loose ends.

items.task: Review your DD-214 CAREFULLY before signing
items.details: This is the most important document of your military career. Check every entry: dates, awards, characterization of service, RE code. Errors are extremely difficult to fix later.
Even if you have other insurance, enroll. You can do this online at va.gov/health-care/apply/. Don't wait.
TAMP: 180 days of premium-free TRICARE after qualifying separations. Retirees: TRICARE Prime/Select continues (enrollment fees apply). Know your coverage start date.
You can leave money in TSP (lowest fees in the industry) or roll to an IRA. Don't cash it out — the tax penalty and lost growth aren't worth it.
SGLI converts to VGLI (if you want it). Update beneficiaries on TSP, life insurance, bank accounts.
Get them while people remember you. COs, department heads, chiefs — anyone who can vouch for your character and competence.

title: You're Out — Now What?
description: The transition doesn't end on your last day. Stay on top of these.

items.task: Verify VA disability rating and payment schedule
items.details: Check eBenefits or va.gov for your rating decision. Payments typically start the month after separation. Disability pay arrives on the 1st, pension on the 1st as well — but they're separate payments.
Apply at va.gov. Also consider getting a VA health ID card at your local VA medical center.

items.task: Register with your state's Department of Veterans Affairs
items.details: State-level benefits (property tax exemptions, hunting/fishing licenses, education programs) often require state VA registration.

items.task: Stay connected with the veteran community
items.details: The camaraderie doesn't have to end. VSOs, veteran groups, online communities — find your people.

items.task: Apply for NGO/organization scholarships for dependents
items.details: Folds of Honor, American Legion Legacy, Fisher House, ThanksUSA — these stack on top of federal and state benefits. Apply to all you qualify for.

items.task: Talk to someone if you need to
items.details: Transition anxiety is real. You don't have to power through alone. Veterans Crisis Line: 988 (press 1). Military OneSource: 800-342-9647.
